content.problemStatement:
Given a `N x N` square matrix, rotate 90° **clockwise**.

For eg. see the following rotation:



Function DeclarationFunction NamerotateClockwiserotateClockwiserotateClockwise

DescriptionrotateClockwiserotateClockwiserotateClockwise : rotates a given **N × N square matrix** by **90 degrees clockwise**.
The rotation should be performed **in-place**, meaning the input matrix itself is modified without using any extra matrix.

Parameters
matrixmatrixmatrix : A 2D array of integers of size N×NN \times NN×N
Represents the square matrix to be rotated.
Return Value
The function **does not return anything**.
The input matrix is modified directly to reflect the rotated matrix.
Constraints
1≤N≤1001 \leq N \leq 1001≤N≤100
The elements of the matrix are non-negative and won't exceed 100010001000.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer NNN, denoting the no. of rows and columns in input matrix
Next NNN lines contains NNN space separated integers, the elements of the matrix.


Output Format

Output NNN lines, each containing NNN space separated integers, the elements of rotated matrix.
**Follow up:** Can you do it in-place by modifying the input matrix, without allocating extra space for another matrix ?
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 1000) – the size of the square matrix. The next N lines each contain N space‑separated integers representing the matrix rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output N lines. Each line contains N space‑separated integers – the matrix after a 90° clockwise rotation. Print the matrices of consecutive test cases one after another without extra blank lines.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first column (3,8,4) becomes the first row reversed (4,8,3), the second column (1,2,6) becomes the second row reversed (6,2,1), and the third column (5,1,0) becomes the third row reversed (0,1,5).
After rotation, element (0,0) moves to (0,1), (0,1) to (1,1), (1,1) to (1,0) and (1,0) to (0,0).
A 1×1 matrix remains unchanged after a 90° clockwise rotation.

solution.approach: To rotate a square matrix 90° clockwise in‑place we use two simple steps. 1) Transpose the matrix – swap element (i, j) with (j, i) for all i < j. After transposition rows become columns. 2) Reverse each row – swapping the first and last element, second and second‑last, etc. After the row reversal the matrix is exactly the original matrix rotated clockwise. Both steps touch each element O(1) times, giving O(N²) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N²) per test case (each of the N² elements is visited a constant number of times).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space – the rotation is performed in‑place.
